metadata.title: Build a Session State File Generator with Claude

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Framing context rot as a workflow failure fixed by three files — manifest, status, handoff — rather than a model limit is something I want to adopt. Can you (1) generate a session-handoff.md in the four-section format with Goal, Done, Next actions, and Do-not-reload; (2) make the Do-not-reload section name actual files, like an already-summarized raw dump, rather than vague categories; (3) show how a fresh session loads only those three files and reports a precise start point in a few thousand tokens?

props.command: Framing context rot as a workflow failure fixed by three files — manifest, status, handoff — rather than a model limit is something I want to adopt. Can you (1) generate a session-handoff.md in the four-section format with Goal, Done, Next actions, and Do-not-reload; (2) make the Do-not-reload section name actual files, like an already-summarized raw dump, rather than vague categories; (3) show how a fresh session loads only those three files and reports a precise start point in a few thousand tokens?
props.segment: Build a Session State File Generator

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Agentic CLI — build it, measure it, take it apart.

Context rot — the session degrading as it accumulates noise — is not a model failure. It's a workflow failure. The fix is three files and a discipline. A manifest, a status file, and a session handoff. Without them, every new session re-reads the same forty megabyte raw dump that was already summarized three sessions ago.

shot.notes: title card: degrading session context visualized as noise accumulating — old files icons piling up; 'context rot = workflow failure, not model failure'; three files highlighted: _MANIFEST.md, status.md, session-handoff.md

beats.narration_text: Ask for the generator. In the terminal: claude — generate a session-handoff.md using the four-section format: Goal, Done, Next actions, Do not reload. Make the Do-not-reload section specific — name actual files, not categories.

props.command:
claude "Generate a session-handoff.md using the format:
  Goal / Done / Next actions / Do not reload.
  Make 'Do not reload' specific — name actual
  files, not categories." < session_notes.txt

beats.narration_text: Read the generator. It accepts a session notes file, calls Claude for each of the three state files in sequence — manifest first, then status, then handoff — and validates the Do-not-reload section for specificity: it must name files, not just topics, and each item must be checkable.

props.code:
# session_state_gen.py — Claude Code output
import subprocess, pathlib, sys

def generate_state(notes_file):
    notes = pathlib.Path(notes_file).read_text()
    for target in ['_MANIFEST.md', 'status.md', 'session-handoff.md']:
        prompt = f"Generate {target} from these session notes.\n"
        if target == 'session-handoff.md':
            prompt += "Include Do-not-reload section naming specific files.\n"
        result = subprocess.run(
            ['claude', prompt + notes],
            capture_output=True, text=True)
        pathlib.Path(target).write_text(result.stdout)
        print(f'wrote {target} ({len(result.stdout)} chars)')

beats.narration_text: The three files generate in sequence. The manifest appears first — project name, canonical files, what the agent should always read. Then status: current blockers, last decision made, branch state. Then handoff: Goal, Done, Next actions numbered starting with a verb, and Do not reload — naming the forty megabyte raw web scrape from July tenth, already summarized into research underscore notes dot md.

shot.notes: terminal showing three files generating sequentially; each file opens in a side pane as markdown; Do-not-reload section highlighted with specific file names, not categories

beats.narration_text: Now simulate starting a new session from the handoff. In the terminal: claude — load only these three state files and tell me where to start, without reading anything else.

props.command:
claude "Load ONLY these three state files and tell me
  exactly where to start the session.
  Do not read anything else." \
  _MANIFEST.md status.md session-handoff.md

beats.narration_text: The agent responds with a precise start-point: the three files loaded, four thousand tokens, and a specific next action — not a question. Compare that to a continued session with four hours of accumulated context: the agent has to reread, re-clarify, and often re-decides something that was already decided.

shot.notes: side-by-side comparison: LEFT 'fresh session + handoff' — 4k tokens, specific next action; RIGHT 'continued session' — 40k tokens, agent re-asking questions; arrow pointing to left with 'more coherent'

beats.narration_text: A fresh session with a good handoff is more coherent than a continued session carrying four hours of accumulated context — because coherence comes from signal density, not from session length. The handoff forces you to compress. The compression is the product.

shot.notes: teardown card: 'coherence = signal density, not session length'; 'the compression IS the product'; three bullets: _MANIFEST.md / status.md / session-handoff.md with check marks

beats.narration_text: Your move: after your next working session, spend five minutes writing the handoff before closing. Next reel: the builder-validator demo — because the same discipline that makes handoffs useful makes validation useful.

shot.notes: next steps: '1) write session-handoff.md after each session  2) validate Do-not-reload has file names  3) start next session from handoff only'; teaser: 'next: builder-validator demo'

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. A fresh session with a good handoff is more coherent than a continued session carrying four hours of accumulated context — because coherence comes from signal density, not from session length. The handoff forces you to compress. The compression is the product. Your move: after your next working session, spend five minutes writing the handoff before closing. Next reel: the builder-validator demo — because the same discipline that makes handoffs useful makes validation useful.
